text: How many meetings do you have?
It's thirteen dollars. It's thirty dollars.
Yes. How much is this one?
The blue one is thirteen ninety-nine.
One-three. Thirteen. Everybody asks me that one. I ask it too, in Spanish.
Thirteen. OK, that's not expensive. How many do you have?
Let me check. I have some in blue, but I don't have any in black.
I need four. Are they available in green?
No, sorry. Only blue. Four blue is fifty-six dollars.
I don't learn fast. I just ask.
I don't have any time on Friday.
I have a question about the bill.

Hi, Paula. David here. You have the Miami trip next month, and before that I want to give you the one thing nobody gives you. It is about numbers. You are going to think this is your problem. It is not. Listen. In English, thirteen and thirty are almost the same word. Fifteen and fifty. Sixteen and sixty. One letter of difference in the sound, and it comes at the end, where nobody is listening anymore. Now, here is what I need you to understand: I am American. I was born with this language. And I mishear it too. Every week. In every currency. So we have a trick, and the trick is not a beginner trick, it is what everyone does. We say the digits. One-three, or three-zero? That is it. That is the whole thing. And when you say it, nobody thinks your English is bad, because it is the same question I ask. There is a rule that helps, and it is small. The teens push the sound to the end: thir-TEEN. The tens push it to the front: THIR-ty. If the end is loud, it is a teen. If the front is loud, it is a ten. It works most of the time, and most of the time is enough, because you have the digits for the rest. One more thing, and this one is your job, not your English. You work with numbers all day. You read a policy in Portuguese and you see a wrong figure in two seconds. That does not go away in English. It is slower, that is all. The number is the last thing that becomes automatic in any language. It is the last thing. Not the hardest, the last. See you Monday.

Good morning, Paula. Sofia here. One thing about Miami, and it is not about English. I have been watching you for three months, and I want to tell you what I see. When a broker gives you a number on the phone, in Portuguese, you repeat it back. Every time. You say the figure out loud and you wait for him to confirm it. I do not think you know you do it. You have done it for twenty years. That is not a habit, Paula, that is the job. In our business a wrong number is not an embarrassment, it is money. It is somebody's factory. So when you get to Miami and the client says a figure and it goes past you, I want you to do exactly what you already do in Portuguese. Say it back. Ask for the digits. Could you repeat that? One-eight-zero? And I want you to notice something while you do it. Nobody at this company has ever lost an account for confirming a figure. Not one. People lose accounts for guessing. For nodding. For writing down eighty when the man said eighteen and finding out in March. So here is the thing I really called to say. You think asking about a number makes you look like a beginner. In this business it makes you look like a professional, and the ones who do not ask are the ones I worry about. You are not going to Miami to prove your English. You are going to get the numbers right. You already know how to do that. See you Monday.
How many nights do you need?
I have some questions about the budget.
I don't have any time on Friday.
I have a question about the bill.
How many people are on your team?
